feedback.text: The propositions of the DFA in its current state bring up a lot of important key issues that are pertinent in protecting consumer rights, particularly in the areas of which these protections are lacking and the consumer remains vulnerable. This initiative seems to seek resolution to numerous issues of exploitation and abuse on the end of businesses, however I believe that more emphasis could be placed on certain practices that are pervading the video-game market-- often touted as the largest growing entertainment market globally. I think it may be important to place a little more/additional emphasis on hardware and software, more specifically dealing with right to repair/modify hardware and ownership permissions/licensing with video games. A lot of the language and premises already contained in the DFA apply strongly to numerous practices within the markets of technological hardware and software, namely video games in terms of software - such as 'dark patterns', 'addictive designs', 'impact on minors', 'unfair practices related to price', 'problems with digital contracts' to name a few. Every week there are more and more examples of businesses trampling consumer rights within the video-game industry. Studios are getting comfortable removing consumers access to video games that they have purchased, meaning both the software and hardware (should the game exist in physical disc format or have peripherals such as a special controller) is terminated and the consumer can no longer access their product. This includes removing access not only to the game itself, but barring access from any (micro)transactions made within the game itself as well as any additional content bonuses or premiums obtained either from purchasing 'special editions' of the game, pre-ordering the game or otherwise separately purchasing additional downloadable content for the game. Companies are instating predatory licensing agreements via EULA that every consumer is forced to sign/agree AFTER PURCHASING, NOT PRIOR, yet signed PRIOR to playing the game itself - in which the licensing agreement explicitly denies consumer-ownership of the product that they have purchased and absolves the company of any wrongdoing should they remove consumer access to their product at any time in the future, for any reason. As a result, due to the crippling lack of legislation to address this: studios are currently operating within the very edge boundary of their legal means to seize products from consumers, products that were sold and advertised as "purchases", "sales"-- not subscriptions or services. These studios are further able to bar access from these purchases at any time, for any reason. For example: a studio's licensing agreement to use models or assets designed by another studio is expiring, and game will not function properly without them nor is it profitable for the studio to renew said license. Henceforth the studio entirely shuts down access to the game, meaning millions of consumers are penalized monetarily (lost access to product their paid for) and their right to use what they purchased is stripped away from them. Additionally the game is not being sold as a service or a product of which the consumer will only have temporary access to. The consumer is not informed that the game is built upon fragile licensing agreements that once terminated, will cause the game to cease to function due to the developers inability or unwillingness (for any reason-- cost or labour) to update the game and allow it to remain in a playable state. The consumer is not given a stated time-frame for which they can access their game nor are they afforded an 'expiration date' for the product. Neither prior to purchasing, prior to signing EULA, or after completion of both is any of this information stated. Instead, studios often turn to "Twitter" or their studio website or news pages within the game itself etc. to inform players post-purchase that they are shutting down. Ran out of space sorry!
